title: Sambe Approach to Floquet-Lindblad Open Quantum Systems
abstract: We study driven and open quantum systems described by a time-periodic Lindblad master equation. In closed systems, the stroboscopic dynamics can always be described by an effective time-independent Floquet Hamiltonian; this idea is the basis of Floquet engineering. However, in the presence of dissipation, the existence of an effective time-independent Floquet Lindbladian is not guaranteed due to the non-unitary nature of the evolution. Using Floquet theory, we construct a well-defined time-independent Floquet Lindbladian in an extended Sambe-Liouville space, transforming the initial time-dependent problem to a static and non-Hermitian eigenvalue problem. For harmonic driving, we introduce a matrix continued fraction method to nonperturbatively resum multiphoton processes and construct an effective Floquet Lindbladian acting only on the physical Liouville space. Compared to other high-frequency expansions, this method has the advantage of providing the whole infinite series expansion at once. Using a resolvent formalism, we show how to obtain a spectral Floquet representation of correlation functions of an open quantum system. As an application, we consider a dissipating two-level system in a linearly polarized field and calculate its resonance fluorescence spectrum. Furthermore, we consider a parametrically driven quantum dot with pump and loss for which we calculate its spectral function and current-voltage characteristics.
summary: This paper develops an advanced theoretical framework to handle periodically driven open quantum systems using Lindblad master equations. It extends established Floquet theory by mapping the problem into an enlarged Sambe-Liouville space, allowing for the construction of an effective time-independent Lindbladian. This method is powerful because it non-perturbatively calculates spectral properties and transport characteristics in regimes where simpler approximations fail.
main_problem: The central problem is extending Floquet engineering concepts, traditionally used for closed quantum systems, to open quantum systems governed by time-periodic Lindblad master equations.
main_result: The authors develop a method using an extended Sambe-Liouville space and matrix continued fractions to construct a well-defined, effective time-independent Floquet Lindbladian acting on the physical Liouville space.
method: Key methods include applying Floquet theory to periodic superoperators, mapping the problem into an enlarged Sambe-Liouville space, and utilizing the matrix continued fraction method for non-perturbative summation of multiphoton processes.
model_or_system: The study concerns open quantum systems driven periodically in time, described by a Lindblad master equation. Applications include dissipating two-level systems (TLS) and parametrically driven quantum dots coupled to leads.
key_observables: Resonance fluorescence spectrum, spectral function, current-voltage characteristics, and correlation functions of the system observables.
important_parameters: Drive frequency ($\omega_0$), coupling strengths ($A$), dissipation rates ($\gamma$), and the ratio $A/\omega_0$ (drive amplitude to frequency).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that a time-independent Floquet Lindbladian can be constructed, which is non-trivial due to the non-unitary nature of open system evolution.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spectrum of the extended Floquet matrix ($\mathcal{L}_F$) and its stroboscopic counterpart ($V(T, 0)$), as well as current-voltage characteristics showing suppression at Bessel function zeros.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the need for an extension of Floquet theory to open systems. It details the Sambe approach in extended space, then introduces the matrix continued fraction method to project onto the physical subspace, and finally applies this framework to calculate spectral properties and transport characteristics.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous theoretical toolset to analyze non-equilibrium steady states in driven quantum matter, bridging the gap between unitary Floquet engineering and dissipative dynamics crucial for quantum optics and solid-state physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2: Spectrum of the stroboscopic evolution oper- ator as obtained from diagonalization of the Floquet- Lindblad matrix LF at different drive frequencies ω0, for A = ∆, γ = 0.1∆, ∆= 1, ϕ = 0.

FIG. 3: Comparison between the Floquet-Lindblad spec- trum obtained from the exact diagonalization of the evolution operator (red dots) and the matrix-continued fraction (black lines). Parameters are chosen as ω0 = ∆/2, γ = 0.01∆, ∆= 1, ϕ = 0.

FIG. 4: (a),(c): Emission spectra of the periodically driven TLS as a function of the emission frequency ω and the amplitude of the drive A in units of the drive frequency ω0. The intensity of the emission is normalized to 1. In (a) the level splitting is zero ∆= 0, while in (c) the drive frequency is resonant to the splitting ∆= ω0. (b): Comparison of the intensity of the emission lines at ω = 0 (top panel) and at ω = ±ω0 (bottom panel) with Bessel functions of the first kind J2 p(2A/ω0) in the case ∆= 0. The rest of the parameters are ω0 = 1, γ = 0.01, ϕ = 0.

FIG. 5: Spectral function A(ω) of the driven resonant level model as a function of the amplitude of the drive A and the frequency ω in units of the drive frequency ω0 obtained through the continued fraction method. The rest of the parameters are set to γL = γR = 0.01, εd = 0.
FIG. 6: Current-voltage characteristics for different driv- ing amplitudes showing the suppression of current at the Bessel function zero of J0(2A/ω0). ω0 = 1, γL = γR = 0.01, εd = 0.
